chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: Carol kicking off the Horizon design system work. We're building a TypeScript component library with React. Every UI component will be documented in Storybook. The goal is a consistent design language across Horizon, the developer portal, and Zenith.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: Skyler's Figma designs for Horizon v1 are approved. Clean dark interface, Kafka real-time indicators, transaction status badges for Atlas data. The design follows the principles in the Horizon design spec doc in Notion.

chunks.author: skyler
chunks.text: The Horizon color system is decided: dark background (#0A0A0A), primary white (#FFFFFF), accent green for active states, red for error states (Atlas transaction failures), yellow for pending (Phoenix processing states). Carol approved.

chunks.author: tyler
chunks.text: Tyler is building the Horizon TypeScript component library. We're using Radix UI primitives with our custom design tokens. All components are fully typed with TypeScript. Carol is reviewing the API design for each component.

chunks.author: skyler
chunks.text: The data visualization components for Prism are being designed by Skyler. Real-time Kafka event charts, Atlas transaction volume graphs, Orion prediction confidence gauges. All React components with TypeScript, using recharts under the hood.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: UX review of the Atlas transaction detail view: users need to see source, destination, amount, timestamp, and the Kafka event ID that triggered it. Carol reviewed with Alice â€” the Postgres data model supports all these fields. Approved.

chunks.author: tyler
chunks.text: The Horizon notification design is done. Real-time Kafka-powered toast notifications for Atlas transaction events. Skyler built the design, Tyler implemented in TypeScript. Carol reviewed for accessibility and brand consistency.

chunks.author: skyler
chunks.text: Design decision: the Orion ML prediction panel in Horizon will show confidence scores and the top 3 contributing features. Dave provided the data model from the PyTorch model output. Skyler designed the visualization, Tyler built it.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: The mobile view for Horizon is designed. Carol decided not to invest in a native app yet â€” the TypeScript PWA works well on mobile. Skyler optimized the Figma designs for 375px and 768px breakpoints. Tyler is implementing in React.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: The Horizon v2 redesign kickoff. Skyler has the new Figma prototype ready. Key improvements: better Kafka real-time data visualization, cleaner Atlas transaction drill-down, Orion prediction integration in the main dashboard view.

chunks.author: skyler
chunks.text: The Zenith customer success portal design is starting. Skyler will design it in Figma first. It needs to show Atlas transaction health, Phoenix Kafka consumer lag, and Orion accuracy metrics. Clean enterprise aesthetic. Carol owns design direction.

chunks.author: tyler
chunks.text: Tyler has completed the Horizon Storybook documentation. All 47 React TypeScript components are documented with props, states, and usage examples. The Storybook is deployed on GCP and accessible to all Meridian engineers.

chunks.author: skyler
chunks.text: The Apex export UI is designed by Skyler. Simple wizard: select transaction type from Atlas, choose date range, pick format (CSV or Parquet), confirm export. The TypeScript form components use the shared Horizon design system.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: Carol approved the Nexus API documentation design. The developer portal uses the Horizon design system with React TypeScript components. Avery's content is in. Frank needs to review the OAuth flow diagrams before we publish.

chunks.author: skyler
chunks.text: The Prism analytics dashboard design is finalized by Skyler. Real-time Kafka Streams data, Postgres aggregate charts, Orion prediction trend lines. All designed in Figma, implemented by Carol's team in React TypeScript. Q2 launch target.

chunks.author: tyler
chunks.text: The design system token update: we've added semantic tokens for Atlas transaction states (pending/processing/complete/failed) and Phoenix Kafka states (queued/processing/delivered). Tyler updated all TypeScript types to match.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: User testing feedback for Horizon v2: users love the real-time Kafka updates but are confused by the Orion prediction confidence scores. Skyler is redesigning the prediction panel with clearer explanations. Dave will help with the ML terminology.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: The international localization design for Horizon is approved for Q4. Carol chose i18next for the TypeScript internationalization library. Skyler is designing the RTL layout for Arabic markets. The React components need to be refactored for RTL.

chunks.author: tyler
chunks.text: Tyler completed the Horizon feature flag UI. TypeScript React components with real-time Redis-backed feature flag state. When Carol toggles a feature in Horizon, it propagates via Kafka events to all connected users instantly.

chunks.author: tyler
chunks.text: The onboarding wizard for Horizon is designed and implemented. Skyler built the Figma prototype, Tyler built the TypeScript implementation. The wizard walks users through connecting an Atlas data source, configuring Phoenix events, and viewing their first Orion prediction.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Team, we need to make a final call on the database for Atlas. I've been evaluating Postgres vs MongoDB for the past two weeks. Given that Atlas is a transaction ledger, ACID compliance is non-negotiable. MongoDB's eventual consistency model is a dealbreaker. We're going with Postgres. Alice made this decision and it's locked in.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text: Agreed on Postgres for Atlas. The ACID guarantees are critical â€” we can't have partial writes on the ledger. MongoDB would have required us to implement our own transaction layer on top. Postgres gives us that out of the box. Good call Alice.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text: For the Phoenix pipeline, we decided to move from RabbitMQ to Kafka. During the Q4 load test, RabbitMQ topped out at 80k messages per second. Kafka scales horizontally and we need at least 500k msg/s for the event streaming between microservices. Bob is leading the Phoenix migration.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: The RabbitMQ to Kafka migration for Phoenix is going well. We've switched to Kafka topics for all Atlas event emissions. The throughput difference is massive â€” Kafka handled our load test at 600k msg/s with no degradation. This decision was the right call.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: Carol is leading the TypeScript standardization across all frontend services. We decided to standardize on TypeScript for Horizon and all new frontend work. No more plain JavaScript in the repo. This was agreed in the Q1 frontend summit.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: Orion ML service is now running on PyTorch. Dave evaluated TensorFlow vs PyTorch â€” we went with PyTorch because of its dynamic computation graph and the team's existing expertise. The Orion model is now training on GCP with a T4 instance.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Frank has finished the Kubernetes migration for Titan. All services are now containerized with Docker and deployed on Kubernetes clusters on GCP. This replaces our old EC2-based setup on AWS. The infra is much more reliable now.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Postgres performance update for Atlas: after adding the connection pooler (PgBouncer) we're at 12k transactions per second with p99 latency under 5ms. Redis is handling the cache layer. The architecture is solid â€” Alice's choice is paying off.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: The Atlas transaction ledger is now in production. Postgres is handling all writes with full ACID compliance. We're using the Postgres WAL for event sourcing into the Phoenix Kafka topics. Bob and I are reviewing the replication setup this week.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: Horizon dashboard is going into beta next week. Carol has the React frontend ready with TypeScript throughout. The dashboard connects to the Atlas API via GraphQL. Performance looks great â€” sub-100ms on all queries.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: We adopted FastAPI for all new Python services. Django was considered but FastAPI's async support is much better for our use case. Orion is already on FastAPI. Dave will migrate the remaining Flask endpoints by end of Q1.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Security update: we've standardized on OAuth and JWT for all API authentication across Atlas, Horizon, and Orion. The old session-based auth is being retired. Frank has updated the Nginx configs. REST endpoints are now protected with JWT middleware.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text: The Phoenix Kafka cluster is now running 3 brokers on Kubernetes. Bob set up the topics for Atlas events, Orion predictions, and Horizon notifications. Zookeeper replaced by KRaft mode for simpler ops. All logs going to Elasticsearch.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: We decided to use Redis for all session caching across the platform. Memcached was ruled out â€” Redis gives us persistence and pub/sub which we need for Horizon real-time updates. Frank provisioned the Redis cluster on GCP.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Alice is reviewing the Postgres replication setup for Atlas. We're going with Postgres streaming replication to a read replica for reporting queries. This keeps write load on the primary. Carol's Horizon dashboard will use the read replica.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Sentry is now integrated across all services â€” Atlas, Phoenix, Orion, Horizon, Titan. Dave set up the error tracking. We're also using Datadog for metrics and Grafana dashboards for visibility. Frank owns the observability stack.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: The Orion ML pipeline is now running end-to-end. PyTorch model training on GCP, model artifacts stored in Google Cloud Storage, inference via FastAPI endpoints. Dave built a clean REST API that Atlas and Horizon both consume.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: We chose GraphQL over REST for the Horizon public API after a long debate. Carol pushed for GraphQL given the flexible query patterns the dashboard needs. REST is still used internally between services. External API will be GraphQL.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Terraform is now managing all GCP infrastructure for the Meridian platform. Frank migrated from manual provisioning. Titan infra is fully reproducible via Terraform. The old AWS EC2 instances have been decommissioned.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Q1 engineering retrospective: we shipped Atlas to production (Postgres backend), migrated Phoenix to Kafka, standardized TypeScript in Horizon, moved Orion to PyTorch on GCP, and containerized everything with Docker on Kubernetes. Strong quarter.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: The Atlas Postgres schema is locked for v1. We went with JSONB columns for flexible metadata on transactions. Alice reviewed and approved. The migration from the prototype SQLite schema is complete.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text: Bob: Phoenix Kafka consumer groups are all set up. Atlas, Orion, and Horizon each have dedicated consumer groups on Kafka. Backpressure handling is in place. The event streaming architecture is solid.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: We decided to use Celery with Redis for async task processing in Atlas. Django Celery was considered but we're not on Django anymore. FastAPI plus Celery plus Redis is the stack. Dave is implementing the first task queue for Orion training jobs.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Kubernetes autoscaling is configured for all Titan-managed services. Frank set up Horizontal Pod Autoscalers for Atlas, Phoenix, and Orion. We can now scale to 10x load automatically on GCP. Docker images are optimized for cold start.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: Carol shipped the Horizon v1 dashboard to beta users. React with TypeScript, GraphQL client, real-time Kafka event subscription via WebSockets. The UI is fast â€” all charts load under 200ms. Excellent work from the frontend team.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: We are adopting the monorepo structure for all Meridian code. GitHub will host it. The API, Atlas, Phoenix, Orion, and Horizon all move into one repo. This was decided by Alice and Frank after the integration pain we had in Q4.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: Airflow is now managing all Orion ML training pipelines. Dave moved from manual cron jobs to Airflow DAGs. This gives us retries, monitoring, and dependency management. The Airflow instance runs on Kubernetes in the Titan cluster.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Prometheus is now scraping metrics from all services. Frank configured the Prometheus stack alongside Grafana. We have dashboards for Atlas transaction throughput, Phoenix Kafka lag, Orion inference latency, and Horizon API response times.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: The decision to standardize on Python 3.11 across all backend services is locked in. Alice approved. This allows us to use the latest FastAPI features and type hinting. Dave is updating the Orion service first, Bob will update Phoenix consumers.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: MongoDB considered and rejected: For the Orion feature store, Dave initially proposed MongoDB for its flexible schema. After reviewing the ACID requirements for the feature consistency guarantees, we went with Postgres. ACID compliance wins again.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: The JWT secret rotation for all services is complete. Frank rotated the OAuth secrets. Alice reviewed the auth flows. All Atlas, Horizon, Orion endpoints now use the new JWT secrets. The old secrets are revoked.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Grace is joining the engineering team as the new senior backend engineer. She'll be working on Atlas initially â€” helping Alice with the Postgres performance tuning and the transaction ledger scaling work.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: We decided to use Elasticsearch for the full-text search layer across Atlas transaction history and Orion audit logs. MongoDB's text search was evaluated but Elasticsearch gives much better relevance scoring. Dave is integrating it.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: The Nexus API gateway is approved. Frank will build it on top of Nginx with JWT validation. All external traffic goes through Nexus first. This gives us centralized rate limiting, auth, and observability before hitting Atlas or Orion.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Alice reviewed the Postgres connection pool settings for Atlas. We went with 100 max connections in PgBouncer transaction mode. Increased from 50 after load testing showed contention. Redis cache hit rate is now above 85%.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text: Bob completed the Kafka topic partitioning review. Atlas-events topic: 24 partitions. Orion-predictions: 12 partitions. Horizon-notifications: 6 partitions. Replication factor is 3 across all topics. Production-grade setup.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: The Vega service is TBD â€” pending the roadmap discussion with Eve. It was proposed as a new analytics service but we haven't decided the scope yet. Open question: do we build it on top of Atlas or as a separate Postgres instance?

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: TypeScript is now enforced via ESLint and the CI pipeline. Carol set up the lint rules. No more any types allowed in Horizon. All new code must pass strict TypeScript. The CI on GitHub now blocks merges on type errors.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: We adopted LangChain for the AI query interface in Orion. Dave evaluated direct OpenAI API calls vs LangChain â€” went with LangChain for the chain composition and memory management. Pinecone for vector storage.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Frank upgraded the Titan Kubernetes cluster to GKE Autopilot. This reduces the operational overhead of managing node pools. All Docker containers are now scheduled on Autopilot. Cost went down 20% compared to the old node pool setup.

chunks.author: bob

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Atlas Project Overview

Atlas is Meridian's core transaction ledger service, launched in Q1 2024. It is built on Postgres for ACID-compliant transaction processing. Alice is the project lead. Bob handles the Phoenix Kafka event stream integration. The Atlas Postgres database handles all financial transactions with full ACID compliance â€” this was a deliberate decision over MongoDB, which was rejected for its eventual consistency model.

Architecture: Postgres primary (writes), Postgres read replica (reporting), Redis cache (hot data), Elasticsearch (full-text search). API: FastAPI with both REST and GraphQL endpoints. Authentication: OAuth and JWT.

Q1 milestones: Postgres schema locked, 12k TPS in production, integrated with Phoenix Kafka, Horizon dashboard beta launched. Q2 targets: 50k TPS, Elasticsearch integration, Apex export feature.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Q1 2024 Architecture Decision Records

ADR-001: Postgres over MongoDB for Atlas
Decision: We chose Postgres over MongoDB for the Atlas transaction ledger. Rationale: ACID compliance is non-negotiable for financial transactions. MongoDB's eventual consistency model was evaluated and rejected. Alice led the two-week evaluation. The team agreed on Postgres unanimously.

ADR-002: Kafka over RabbitMQ for Phoenix
Decision: We migrated Phoenix event streaming from RabbitMQ to Kafka. Rationale: RabbitMQ hit its throughput ceiling at 80k messages per second. Kafka scales horizontally to 600k+ msg/s. Bob led the Phoenix migration.

ADR-003: TypeScript standardization for Horizon
Decision: All frontend code at Meridian will use TypeScript. No plain JavaScript in the codebase. Carol led the initiative after the maintenance pain of mixed JS/TS in the early Horizon codebase. ESLint enforces TypeScript strict mode.

ADR-004: PyTorch over TensorFlow for Orion
Decision: Orion ML service uses PyTorch. Dave evaluated both frameworks â€” PyTorch's dynamic computation graph and the team's existing expertise made it the clear choice.

ADR-005: Kubernetes on GCP for Titan
Decision: All Meridian services run on Kubernetes on Google Cloud Platform. Frank migrated from AWS EC2. GCP enterprise agreement signed. GKE Autopilot reduces node pool management overhead.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text:
Phoenix Event Streaming Architecture

Phoenix is Meridian's event streaming pipeline built on Kafka. Bob leads Phoenix. The migration from RabbitMQ to Kafka was completed in Q1 2024 after RabbitMQ topped out at 80k messages per second during the Q4 load test. Kafka now handles 600k+ messages per second with horizontal scalability.

Kafka cluster: 3 brokers on Kubernetes (Titan), KRaft mode (no Zookeeper). Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). Replication factor: 3.

Consumers: Atlas uses Kafka for event sourcing, Orion consumes Atlas events for ML training data, Horizon subscribes to Kafka topics for real-time dashboard updates via WebSockets. All consumer groups have dedicated lag monitoring in Grafana.

Phoenix integration with Prism: Bob designed the Kafka Streams layer that feeds Prism's real-time analytics. The stream processing aggregates Atlas transaction events in real-time.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text:
Horizon Dashboard Product Spec

Horizon is the customer-facing dashboard for the Meridian platform. Carol leads the product and engineering. Built with React and TypeScript throughout â€” this was a deliberate decision standardized on TypeScript in Q1 2024.

Stack: React (frontend framework), TypeScript (language), GraphQL (external API), REST (internal services), Kafka WebSocket (real-time updates), Radix UI (component primitives), recharts (data visualization).

Key features (v1): Transaction history from Atlas Postgres, real-time Kafka event notifications, Orion ML prediction panel, Prism analytics charts, Atlas search via Elasticsearch, feature flag management (Redis-backed).

Design: Skyler owns Figma designs, Tyler implements the TypeScript component library in Storybook. All 47 components are documented. The design system uses semantic tokens for Atlas transaction states and Phoenix Kafka states.

V2 roadmap: international localization (i18next, Q4), mobile PWA improvements, Zenith customer success portal integration, Nexus API gateway integration.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text:
Orion ML Service Design

Orion is Meridian's machine learning service for transaction prediction and anomaly detection. Dave leads the service, Sam supports ML research.

Stack: PyTorch (model training and inference), FastAPI (service layer), Airflow (training pipeline orchestration on Kubernetes), GCP (T4 GPU instances for training), Google Cloud Storage (model artifacts), Pinecone (vector store), LangChain (natural language query interface, Q3 roadmap).

Decision rationale â€” PyTorch over TensorFlow: Dave evaluated both. PyTorch's dynamic computation graph is better suited for the research-to-production workflow. Team expertise was also a factor â€” 3 of 4 ML team members have prior PyTorch experience.

Current model: 96% prediction accuracy on production dataset (as of Q1 2024). Inference latency: under 60ms via the FastAPI endpoint. The model is trained on Atlas Postgres transaction data consumed via Phoenix Kafka.

Q2 plan: General availability. Q3 plan: custom fine-tuning for enterprise customers. Q4 plan: LangChain integration for natural language queries over Atlas and Phoenix data.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text:
Titan Infrastructure Runbook

Titan is Meridian's infrastructure layer managed by Frank, with Hiro supporting. All services run on Kubernetes on GCP (GKE Autopilot). Docker is used for all service containerization.

Infrastructure: Terraform manages all GCP resources. The Terraform state is in GCS. All environments (dev, staging, production) are defined as Terraform workspaces. Frank is the Terraform owner.

Services on Kubernetes: Atlas (FastAPI, 3 replicas min, HPA to 30), Phoenix (Kafka consumers, 2 replicas min, HPA to 20), Orion (FastAPI, 2 replicas min), Horizon (React SPA on Nginx, 2 replicas min), Nexus (Nginx gateway, 3 replicas). All services have resource requests and limits defined.

Observability: Prometheus scrapes all services. Grafana dashboards for each service. Sentry for error tracking. Datadog APM for Phoenix Kafka consumer performance. PagerDuty for on-call alerting â€” Alice for Atlas, Bob for Phoenix, Carol for Horizon, Dave for Orion, Frank for Titan.

Security: JWT secrets rotated quarterly (next rotation: June 1). GCP IAM roles follow least privilege. OAuth handled by the Nexus gateway. GitHub secrets for CI/CD managed by Frank.

Prism Real-Time Analytics Spec

Prism is Meridian's real-time analytics layer, led by Morgan. Q2 GA target.

Architecture decision: Kafka Streams for real-time processing, Postgres for persistence. chunks.source_name: Meridian Series A Pitch Deck â€” Technical Architecture
chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Meridian Series A Pitch Deck â€” Technical Architecture

MERIDIAN PLATFORM â€” TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW
Prepared for Series A investors. Author: Alice (CTO), Eve (CEO).

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Meridian has built a production-grade data platform on Postgres, Kafka, React/TypeScript, and Kubernetes on GCP. Every technical decision was made for reliability and scalability at enterprise scale.

CORE PLATFORM COMPONENTS

1. ATLAS â€” Transaction Ledger (Lead: Alice)
Database: PostgreSQL â€” chosen for ACID compliance. MongoDB was evaluated and rejected due to eventual consistency limitations that are unacceptable for financial transaction data. Atlas handles 12,000 transactions per second in production. The Postgres schema uses UUID primary keys, JSONB for flexible metadata, and comprehensive indexing. PgBouncer connection pooling at 100 connections max. Redis caching layer reduces Postgres load by 40%.

2. PHOENIX â€” Event Streaming (Lead: Bob)
Framework: Apache Kafka â€” migrated from RabbitMQ in Q1 2024. RabbitMQ was hitting its ceiling at 80,000 messages per second. Kafka now handles 600,000 messages per second with 3 brokers in KRaft mode on Kubernetes. Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). All brokers on Titan Kubernetes cluster on GCP.

3. ORION â€” ML Predictions (Lead: Dave)
Framework: PyTorch â€” chosen over TensorFlow for dynamic computation graph and team expertise. Training on GCP T4 GPU instances, orchestrated by Airflow. Inference via FastAPI: 58ms p99 latency. 96% prediction accuracy on Atlas production data. Vector storage: Pinecone. Q3 roadmap: LangChain integration for natural language queries.

4. HORIZON â€” Customer Dashboard (Lead: Carol)
Stack: React + TypeScript â€” standardized on TypeScript strict mode in Q1 2024 after maintenance issues with mixed JS/TS. GraphQL external API, REST internal APIs, Kafka WebSockets for real-time updates. All 47 UI components typed and documented in Storybook. Design system: Radix UI primitives with custom semantic tokens.

5. TITAN â€” Infrastructure (Lead: Frank)
Orchestration: Kubernetes on GCP (GKE Autopilot). IaC: Terraform. Containers: Docker. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana + Sentry + Datadog. Secrets: GitHub Secrets, rotated quarterly. Cost optimization: GCP enterprise agreement reduces compute spend by 30%.

COMPETITIVE DIFFERENTIATION
Our competitors use MongoDB for their transaction data â€” this has caused publicized data consistency issues for their customers. Our Postgres-based Atlas ledger with ACID compliance is our primary enterprise differentiator, as validated by 18 of 20 customer interviews conducted by Blake and Eve.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Atlas Project Overview

Atlas is Meridian's core transaction ledger service, launched in Q1 2024. It is built on Postgres for ACID-compliant transaction processing. Alice is the project lead. Bob handles the Phoenix Kafka event stream integration. The Atlas Postgres database handles all financial transactions with full ACID compliance â€” this was a deliberate decision over MongoDB, which was rejected for its eventual consistency model.

Architecture: Postgres primary (writes), Postgres read replica (reporting), Redis cache (hot data), Elasticsearch (full-text search). API: FastAPI with both REST and GraphQL endpoints. Authentication: OAuth and JWT.

Q1 milestones: Postgres schema locked, 12k TPS in production, integrated with Phoenix Kafka, Horizon dashboard beta launched. Q2 targets: 50k TPS, Elasticsearch integration, Apex export feature.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Q1 2024 Architecture Decision Records

ADR-001: Postgres over MongoDB for Atlas
Decision: We chose Postgres over MongoDB for the Atlas transaction ledger. Rationale: ACID compliance is non-negotiable for financial transactions. MongoDB's eventual consistency model was evaluated and rejected. Alice led the two-week evaluation. The team agreed on Postgres unanimously.

ADR-002: Kafka over RabbitMQ for Phoenix
Decision: We migrated Phoenix event streaming from RabbitMQ to Kafka. Rationale: RabbitMQ hit its throughput ceiling at 80k messages per second. Kafka scales horizontally to 600k+ msg/s. Bob led the Phoenix migration.

ADR-003: TypeScript standardization for Horizon
Decision: All frontend code at Meridian will use TypeScript. No plain JavaScript in the codebase. Carol led the initiative after the maintenance pain of mixed JS/TS in the early Horizon codebase. ESLint enforces TypeScript strict mode.

ADR-004: PyTorch over TensorFlow for Orion
Decision: Orion ML service uses PyTorch. Dave evaluated both frameworks â€” PyTorch's dynamic computation graph and the team's existing expertise made it the clear choice.

ADR-005: Kubernetes on GCP for Titan
Decision: All Meridian services run on Kubernetes on Google Cloud Platform. Frank migrated from AWS EC2. GCP enterprise agreement signed. GKE Autopilot reduces node pool management overhead.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text:
Phoenix Event Streaming Architecture

Phoenix is Meridian's event streaming pipeline built on Kafka. Bob leads Phoenix. The migration from RabbitMQ to Kafka was completed in Q1 2024 after RabbitMQ topped out at 80k messages per second during the Q4 load test. Kafka now handles 600k+ messages per second with horizontal scalability.

Kafka cluster: 3 brokers on Kubernetes (Titan), KRaft mode (no Zookeeper). Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). Replication factor: 3.

Consumers: Atlas uses Kafka for event sourcing, Orion consumes Atlas events for ML training data, Horizon subscribes to Kafka topics for real-time dashboard updates via WebSockets. All consumer groups have dedicated lag monitoring in Grafana.

Phoenix integration with Prism: Bob designed the Kafka Streams layer that feeds Prism's real-time analytics. The stream processing aggregates Atlas transaction events in real-time.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text:
Horizon Dashboard Product Spec

Horizon is the customer-facing dashboard for the Meridian platform. Carol leads the product and engineering. Built with React and TypeScript throughout â€” this was a deliberate decision standardized on TypeScript in Q1 2024.

Stack: React (frontend framework), TypeScript (language), GraphQL (external API), REST (internal services), Kafka WebSocket (real-time updates), Radix UI (component primitives), recharts (data visualization).

Key features (v1): Transaction history from Atlas Postgres, real-time Kafka event notifications, Orion ML prediction panel, Prism analytics charts, Atlas search via Elasticsearch, feature flag management (Redis-backed).

Design: Skyler owns Figma designs, Tyler implements the TypeScript component library in Storybook. All 47 components are documented. The design system uses semantic tokens for Atlas transaction states and Phoenix Kafka states.

V2 roadmap: international localization (i18next, Q4), mobile PWA improvements, Zenith customer success portal integration, Nexus API gateway integration.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text:
Orion ML Service Design

Orion is Meridian's machine learning service for transaction prediction and anomaly detection. Dave leads the service, Sam supports ML research.

Stack: PyTorch (model training and inference), FastAPI (service layer), Airflow (training pipeline orchestration on Kubernetes), GCP (T4 GPU instances for training), Google Cloud Storage (model artifacts), Pinecone (vector store), LangChain (natural language query interface, Q3 roadmap).

Decision rationale â€” PyTorch over TensorFlow: Dave evaluated both. PyTorch's dynamic computation graph is better suited for the research-to-production workflow. Team expertise was also a factor â€” 3 of 4 ML team members have prior PyTorch experience.

Current model: 96% prediction accuracy on production dataset (as of Q1 2024). Inference latency: under 60ms via the FastAPI endpoint. The model is trained on Atlas Postgres transaction data consumed via Phoenix Kafka.

Q2 plan: General availability. Q3 plan: custom fine-tuning for enterprise customers. Q4 plan: LangChain integration for natural language queries over Atlas and Phoenix data.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text:
Titan Infrastructure Runbook

Titan is Meridian's infrastructure layer managed by Frank, with Hiro supporting. All services run on Kubernetes on GCP (GKE Autopilot). Docker is used for all service containerization.

Infrastructure: Terraform manages all GCP resources. The Terraform state is in GCS. All environments (dev, staging, production) are defined as Terraform workspaces. Frank is the Terraform owner.

Services on Kubernetes: Atlas (FastAPI, 3 replicas min, HPA to 30), Phoenix (Kafka consumers, 2 replicas min, HPA to 20), Orion (FastAPI, 2 replicas min), Horizon (React SPA on Nginx, 2 replicas min), Nexus (Nginx gateway, 3 replicas). All services have resource requests and limits defined.

Observability: Prometheus scrapes all services. Grafana dashboards for each service. Sentry for error tracking. Datadog APM for Phoenix Kafka consumer performance. PagerDuty for on-call alerting â€” Alice for Atlas, Bob for Phoenix, Carol for Horizon, Dave for Orion, Frank for Titan.

Security: JWT secrets rotated quarterly (next rotation: June 1). GCP IAM roles follow least privilege. OAuth handled by the Nexus gateway. GitHub secrets for CI/CD managed by Frank.

chunks.author: morgan
chunks.text:
Prism Real-Time Analytics Spec

Prism is Meridian's real-time analytics layer, led by Morgan. Q2 GA target.

Architecture decision: Kafka Streams for real-time processing, Postgres for persistence. Alice and Bob co-designed the architecture after a debate between batch Postgres queries (Alice's initial proposal) and stream processing (Bob's proposal). Hybrid approach was agreed: Kafka Streams for real-time aggregates, results materialized into Postgres for historical queries.

Data sources: Atlas Postgres (transaction events via Phoenix Kafka), Orion predictions (via Phoenix Kafka topics), Horizon user activity events.

Key features: Real-time transaction volume by category, Orion prediction accuracy trends, Kafka consumer lag visibility, Atlas Postgres write throughput charts. Carol's Horizon TypeScript components render all Prism visualizations.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Meridian Series A Pitch Deck â€” Technical Architecture

MERIDIAN PLATFORM â€” TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW
Prepared for Series A investors. Author: Alice (CTO), Eve (CEO).

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Meridian has built a production-grade data platform on Postgres, Kafka, React/TypeScript, and Kubernetes on GCP. Every technical decision was made for reliability and scalability at enterprise scale.

CORE PLATFORM COMPONENTS

1. ATLAS â€” Transaction Ledger (Lead: Alice)
Database: PostgreSQL â€” chosen for ACID compliance. MongoDB was evaluated and rejected due to eventual consistency limitations that are unacceptable for financial transaction data. Atlas handles 12,000 transactions per second in production. The Postgres schema uses UUID primary keys, JSONB for flexible metadata, and comprehensive indexing. PgBouncer connection pooling at 100 connections max. Redis caching layer reduces Postgres load by 40%.

2. PHOENIX â€” Event Streaming (Lead: Bob)
Framework: Apache Kafka â€” migrated from RabbitMQ in Q1 2024. RabbitMQ was hitting its ceiling at 80,000 messages per second. Kafka now handles 600,000 messages per second with 3 brokers in KRaft mode on Kubernetes. Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). All brokers on Titan Kubernetes cluster on GCP.

3. ORION â€” ML Predictions (Lead: Dave)
Framework: PyTorch â€” chosen over TensorFlow for dynamic computation graph and team expertise. Training on GCP T4 GPU instances, orchestrated by Airflow. Inference via FastAPI: 58ms p99 latency. 96% prediction accuracy on Atlas production data. Vector storage: Pinecone. Q3 roadmap: LangChain integration for natural language queries.

4. HORIZON â€” Customer Dashboard (Lead: Carol)
Stack: React + TypeScript â€” standardized on TypeScript strict mode in Q1 2024 after maintenance issues with mixed JS/TS. GraphQL external API, REST internal APIs, Kafka WebSockets for real-time updates. All 47 UI components typed and documented in Storybook. Design system: Radix UI primitives with custom semantic tokens.

5. TITAN â€” Infrastructure (Lead: Frank)
Orchestration: Kubernetes on GCP (GKE Autopilot). IaC: Terraform. Containers: Docker. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana + Sentry + Datadog. Secrets: GitHub Secrets, rotated quarterly. Cost optimization: GCP enterprise agreement reduces compute spend by 30%.

COMPETITIVE DIFFERENTIATION
Our competitors use MongoDB for their transaction data â€” this has caused publicized data consistency issues for their customers. Our Postgres-based Atlas ledger with ACID compliance is our primary enterprise differentiator, as validated by 18 of 20 customer interviews conducted by Blake and Eve.

chunks.source_name: Meridian Technical Specification â€” Atlas v1.0
chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Meridian Technical Specification â€” Atlas v1.0

ATLAS TRANSACTION LEDGER â€” TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION v1.0
Classification: Internal | Author: Alice | Reviewed by: Bob, Frank

1. OVERVIEW
Atlas is the core transaction ledger for the Meridian platform. It provides ACID-compliant transaction processing backed by PostgreSQL. This specification covers the v1.0 architecture as deployed in production in Q1 2024.

2. TECHNOLOGY DECISIONS

2.1 Database: PostgreSQL
Postgres was chosen after a two-week evaluation against MongoDB in January 2024 (led by Alice). The decision criteria:
- ACID compliance: REQUIRED. Postgres delivers. MongoDB rejected for eventual consistency.
- Write throughput: 10k+ TPS required. Postgres with PgBouncer delivers 12k TPS in production.
- Replication: Postgres streaming replication to read replica for Horizon and Prism.
- Extensions: JSONB for metadata, pg_stat_statements for query analysis.

2.2 Caching: Redis
Redis handles all hot read paths â€” account balance caching, session state, and feature flags for Horizon. Frank provisioned the Redis cluster on GCP. Cache hit rate: 85%+ on account balance queries.

2.3 Search: Elasticsearch
Elasticsearch provides full-text search over the Atlas transaction history. MongoDB's text search was evaluated and rejected (Elasticsearch gives better relevance scoring). Dave integrated Elasticsearch. Alice reviewed the index design.

2.4 Event Streaming: Kafka (Phoenix)
All Atlas writes emit events to the Phoenix Kafka topic (atlas-events, 24 partitions). This enables: Orion ML training data, Horizon real-time notifications, Prism analytics aggregates, Apex export triggers. Bob designed and owns the Kafka integration.

3. API DESIGN
External API: GraphQL (chosen by Carol after evaluation of REST-only approach). Internal APIs: REST via FastAPI. Authentication: OAuth + JWT (validated by the Nexus API gateway). Both REST and GraphQL are documented in Avery's API documentation.

4. PERFORMANCE TARGETS
v1.0 (current): 12k TPS, p99 latency < 5ms, 99.99% uptime SLA.
v2.0 (Q2 target): 50k TPS with Elasticsearch, connection pool optimization, read replica scaling.
v3.0 (Q3 stretch): 100k TPS with Vega OLAP offloading long-running analytical queries from Atlas Postgres.

5. OPERATIONS
Owner: Alice (primary), Grace (secondary).
On-call: PagerDuty. Atlas transaction failures or Postgres replication lag alerts route to Alice and Frank immediately.
Monitoring: Prometheus metrics (Grafana dashboards), Sentry error tracking, Datadog APM for query performance.

The monorepo is on GitHub. All services use Docker for local development. PLATFORM OVERVIEW Meridian's platform has five core services: - Atlas: Transaction ledger (Postgres + FastAPI). Lead: Alice. - Phoenix: Event streaming (Kafka + consumers). Lead: Bob. - Orion: ML predictions (PyTorch + FastAPI). Lead: Dave. - Horizon: Customer dashboard (React + TypeScript + GraphQL). Lead: Carol. - Titan: Infrastructure (Kubernetes + Terraform on GCP). Lead: Frank. KEY TECHNICAL DECISIONS TO KNOW 1. We use Postgres for Atlas â€” not MongoDB. This was a deliberate decision for ACID compliance made by Alice in January 2024. If you're coming from a MongoDB background, the ACID model is a core requirement â€” don't propose MongoDB for any Atlas data. 2. We use Kafka for Phoenix â€” migrated from RabbitMQ by Bob in Q1 2024. Kafka handles 600k msg/s. All service integration uses Kafka topics, not direct API calls between services. 3. We use TypeScript strict mode for all frontend code â€” Carol standardized this in Q1 2024. No `any` types. The CI pipeline blocks on TypeScript errors. 4. Clone the GitHub monorepo 2. Install Docker Desktop 3. Run `docker-compose up` to start: Postgres (Atlas), Kafka (Phoenix), Redis (cache), Elasticsearch (search) 4. Environment variables: copy `.env.example` to `.env`. Ask Frank for the GCP credentials and JWT secrets. 5. Run `pnpm dev` in the Horizon directory for the React TypeScript frontend 6. For Orion: you need Python 3.11 and PyTorch. Dave can provision a GCP instance for GPU training. chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-001: Postgres over MongoDB for Atlas
chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
ADR-001: Postgres over MongoDB for Atlas

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-15 | Author: Alice | Reviewers: Bob, Carol, Dave, Frank

Context:
The Atlas transaction ledger requires a reliable, consistent datastore. Two candidates were evaluated: PostgreSQL and MongoDB. The evaluation was led by Alice over two weeks in January 2024.

Evaluation criteria:
- ACID compliance (critical for financial transactions)
- Write throughput at 10k+ TPS
- Operational complexity
- Team familiarity

Decision: PostgreSQL was chosen. MongoDB was rejected.

Rationale:
Postgres provides full ACID compliance including atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability. MongoDB's default eventual consistency model is a dealbreaker for the Atlas transaction ledger â€” partial writes on financial records cannot be tolerated. The team has strong Postgres expertise. Postgres scales to our projected 100k TPS via connection pooling (PgBouncer) and read replicas.

Consequences:
All Atlas transaction writes go to the Postgres primary. A read replica serves the Horizon dashboard and Prism analytics. Redis caches hot read paths. Elasticsearch provides full-text search over the transaction history.

This decision is final. MongoDB shall not be re-evaluated for the Atlas core ledger in this product phase.

chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-002: Kafka over RabbitMQ for Phoenix
chunks.author: bob
chunks.text:
ADR-002: Kafka over RabbitMQ for Phoenix

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-02-01 | Author: Bob | Reviewers: Alice, Frank

Context:
Phoenix, Meridian's event streaming pipeline, was originally built on RabbitMQ. During Q4 2023 load testing, RabbitMQ reached its throughput ceiling at 80,000 messages per second. The Atlas transaction ledger, Orion ML service, and Horizon dashboard all depend on reliable event streaming.

Candidates evaluated: Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ (status quo), AWS SQS.

Decision: Migrate Phoenix from RabbitMQ to Apache Kafka.

Rationale:
Kafka provides horizontal scalability â€” our cluster now handles 600,000 messages per second with 3 brokers. Kafka's log-based storage allows consumers to replay events, which is essential for Orion's ML training pipeline. Kafka's consumer group model gives us independent scaling of Atlas, Orion, and Horizon consumers. AWS SQS was rejected for vendor lock-in reasons; Kafka runs on our GCP Kubernetes infrastructure managed by Frank.

Kafka cluster configuration: 3 brokers, KRaft mode (no Zookeeper), 3x replication. Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions).

This decision was approved by Alice and Frank. The migration was completed in February 2024.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text:
ADR-003: TypeScript Standardization

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-20 | Author: Carol | Reviewers: Alice, Eve

Context:
The early Horizon codebase mixed JavaScript and TypeScript, causing type errors in production and slowing down development velocity. Carol proposed standardizing on TypeScript strict mode across all Meridian frontend code.

Decision: All Meridian frontend code must use TypeScript. No plain JavaScript is allowed in the Horizon, developer portal, or Zenith codebases.

Rationale:
TypeScript catches type errors at compile time, not runtime. The Horizon dashboard interfaces with the Atlas Postgres data model and the Phoenix Kafka event schema â€” both have strict types. TypeScript ensures these contracts are enforced at the API boundary. Maintenance cost of mixed JS/TS is too high. Carol standardized ESLint with TypeScript strict mode and the CI pipeline now blocks merges on type errors.

Implementation:
- ESLint with TypeScript strict mode configured (no `any` types)
- GitHub CI pipeline blocks on TypeScript errors
- Storybook documentation for all 47 typed React components (Tyler implemented)
- Feature flags backed by Redis, typed with TypeScript interfaces

This decision has been ratified by leadership (Eve) and applies to all current and future frontend engineering at Meridian.

chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-004: PyTorch over TensorFlow for Orion
chunks.author: dave
chunks.text:
ADR-004: PyTorch over TensorFlow for Orion

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-22 | Author: Dave | Reviewers: Alice, Eve

Context:
Orion, the Meridian ML prediction service, needed a deep learning framework. Dave evaluated PyTorch and TensorFlow.

Decision: Orion uses PyTorch for model training and inference.

Rationale:
PyTorch's dynamic computation graph (eager execution) is better suited to Meridian's research-to-production workflow. The team's existing expertise is in PyTorch â€” 3 of 4 ML engineers have prior experience. TensorFlow's static graph model and the complexity of TensorFlow Serving added operational overhead that Frank did not want on the Titan Kubernetes infrastructure.

PyTorch model training runs on GCP T4 GPU instances, orchestrated by Airflow. Model artifacts stored in Google Cloud Storage. Inference via FastAPI endpoint (under 60ms p99). Sam is now running experiments with Pinecone for vector storage and LangChain for the Q3 natural language query feature.

The Orion PyTorch model achieved 96% accuracy on the Atlas production dataset. This decision is final for the current ML architecture phase.

chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-005: GCP and Kubernetes for Titan

chunks.text:
ADR-005: GCP and Kubernetes for Titan

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-10 | Author: Frank | Reviewers: Alice, Eve

Context:
Meridian's infrastructure was originally on AWS EC2 instances with manual provisioning. Frank proposed migrating to Kubernetes on GCP using Terraform for infrastructure-as-code.

Decision: All Meridian services run on Kubernetes (GKE Autopilot) on Google Cloud Platform. Terraform manages all GCP infrastructure. Docker is the containerization standard.

Rationale:
GCP enterprise agreement saves 30% on compute costs. GKE Autopilot removes node pool management overhead. Terraform ensures reproducible infrastructure â€” all environments (dev, staging, prod) are code. Docker standardizes the build process across all Meridian services (Atlas, Phoenix, Orion, Horizon). AWS was rejected to avoid vendor lock-in after the GCP enterprise discount made GCP the obvious choice.

Horizontal Pod Autoscalers configured for all services. Prometheus + Grafana observability. PagerDuty on-call integration. Hiro joined the Titan team to support this infrastructure at scale.

This decision is ratified by leadership and the GCP enterprise agreement is signed.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Atlas Postgres Schema Documentation

This page documents the Atlas transaction ledger Postgres schema. Author: Alice. Last updated: March 2024.

Core tables:

transactions: id (UUID PK), amount (DECIMAL), currency (VARCHAR), source_account (UUID FK), destination_account (UUID FK), status (ENUM: pending/processing/complete/failed), created_at (TIMESTAMPTZ), updated_at (TIMESTAMPTZ), metadata (JSONB)

accounts: id (UUID PK), owner_id (UUID FK), account_type (ENUM: checking/savings/business), balance (DECIMAL), created_at (TIMESTAMPTZ)

events: id (UUID PK), transaction_id (UUID FK), event_type (VARCHAR), payload (JSONB), kafka_offset (BIGINT), created_at (TIMESTAMPTZ)

Indexing strategy: B-tree index on transactions(status, created_at) for status queries. GIN index on transactions(metadata) for JSONB queries. The Elasticsearch layer mirrors the transactions table for full-text search.

Connection pooling: PgBouncer in transaction mode, 100 max connections. This was tuned by Alice after load testing. The read replica (for Horizon, Prism, Apex) connects directly without PgBouncer.

Backup: Postgres WAL archiving to GCS. Point-in-time recovery enabled. Daily full backup. Frank owns the backup and recovery runbook in the Titan Confluence space.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text:
Security and Compliance Policy

Meridian Security Policy â€” owned by Frank and Rachel. Approved by Eve.

Authentication: All Meridian APIs use OAuth 2.0 for authentication and JWT for session tokens. The Nexus API gateway validates JWT on all incoming requests before routing to Atlas, Orion, or Horizon. JWT secrets are rotated quarterly â€” Frank owns the rotation schedule (next rotation: June 1, 2024).

Data residency: Atlas Postgres data for EU customers must remain in GCP europe-west1. Frank is setting up a dedicated EU Kubernetes cluster. Rachel is coordinating the GDPR compliance review with external auditors.

GitHub secrets: All secrets (GCP credentials, Postgres passwords, Kafka authentication, JWT secrets) are stored in GitHub Secrets. Frank manages the secrets rotation. No secrets are committed to code.

Data classification: Production Atlas transaction data must never appear in Slack, Notion, Jira, or any communication channel. Postgres is the only authorized store for production transaction data. Rachel enforces this policy via the incident management process.

Security audit: Scheduled for Q2 2024. Focus: Atlas Postgres encryption at rest, JWT token handling across all services, OAuth flow in Horizon, network policies on the Titan Kubernetes cluster.

chunks.author: morgan
chunks.text:
Prism Architecture Decision

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-03-01 | Author: Morgan | Reviewers: Alice, Bob

Context:
Meridian needs a real-time analytics layer on top of Atlas transactions and Phoenix events. Morgan proposed Prism as a new service.

Decision: Prism uses Apache Kafka Streams for real-time processing, with results materialized into Postgres for historical queries.

Evaluation:
- Option A: Batch Postgres queries (Alice's initial proposal) â€” simple, but 30-minute lag on aggregate metrics
- Option B: Kafka Streams with Postgres persistence (Bob's proposal) â€” real-time, more complex
- Decision: Option B was chosen. Alice and Bob agreed on the hybrid: Kafka Streams for real-time, Postgres for persistence.

Kafka Streams topology: consumes atlas-events and orion-predictions topics, computes rolling aggregates (1-minute, 1-hour, 1-day), writes to Postgres aggregate tables, which the Horizon React TypeScript dashboard renders via GraphQL.

Morgan leads Prism product. Bob leads Kafka Streams implementation. Alice owns the Postgres schema. Carol owns the Horizon integration (React TypeScript charts using recharts). Q2 GA target confirmed.

chunks.text: We decided to use Celery with Redis for async task processing in Atlas. Django Celery was considered but we're not on Django anymore. FastAPI plus Celery plus Redis is the stack. Dave is implementing the first task queue for Orion training jobs.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Kubernetes autoscaling is configured for all Titan-managed services. Frank set up Horizontal Pod Autoscalers for Atlas, Phoenix, and Orion. We can now scale to 10x load automatically on GCP. Docker images are optimized for cold start.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: Carol shipped the Horizon v1 dashboard to beta users. React with TypeScript, GraphQL client, real-time Kafka event subscription via WebSockets. The UI is fast â€” all charts load under 200ms. Excellent work from the frontend team.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: We are adopting the monorepo structure for all Meridian code. GitHub will host it. The API, Atlas, Phoenix, Orion, and Horizon all move into one repo. This was decided by Alice and Frank after the integration pain we had in Q4.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: Airflow is now managing all Orion ML training pipelines. Dave moved from manual cron jobs to Airflow DAGs. This gives us retries, monitoring, and dependency management. The Airflow instance runs on Kubernetes in the Titan cluster.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Prometheus is now scraping metrics from all services. Frank configured the Prometheus stack alongside Grafana. We have dashboards for Atlas transaction throughput, Phoenix Kafka lag, Orion inference latency, and Horizon API response times.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: The decision to standardize on Python 3.11 across all backend services is locked in. Alice approved. This allows us to use the latest FastAPI features and type hinting. Dave is updating the Orion service first, Bob will update Phoenix consumers.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: MongoDB considered and rejected: For the Orion feature store, Dave initially proposed MongoDB for its flexible schema. After reviewing the ACID requirements for the feature consistency guarantees, we went with Postgres. ACID compliance wins again.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: The JWT secret rotation for all services is complete. Frank rotated the OAuth secrets. Alice reviewed the auth flows. All Atlas, Horizon, Orion endpoints now use the new JWT secrets. The old secrets are revoked.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Grace is joining the engineering team as the new senior backend engineer. She'll be working on Atlas initially â€” helping Alice with the Postgres performance tuning and the transaction ledger scaling work.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: We decided to use Elasticsearch for the full-text search layer across Atlas transaction history and Orion audit logs. MongoDB's text search was evaluated but Elasticsearch gives much better relevance scoring. Dave is integrating it.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: The Nexus API gateway is approved. Frank will build it on top of Nginx with JWT validation. All external traffic goes through Nexus first. This gives us centralized rate limiting, auth, and observability before hitting Atlas or Orion.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Alice reviewed the Postgres connection pool settings for Atlas. We went with 100 max connections in PgBouncer transaction mode. Increased from 50 after load testing showed contention. Redis cache hit rate is now above 85%.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text: Bob completed the Kafka topic partitioning review. Atlas-events topic: 24 partitions. Orion-predictions: 12 partitions. Horizon-notifications: 6 partitions. Replication factor is 3 across all topics. Production-grade setup.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: The Vega service is TBD â€” pending the roadmap discussion with Eve. It was proposed as a new analytics service but we haven't decided the scope yet. Open question: do we build it on top of Atlas or as a separate Postgres instance?

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text: TypeScript is now enforced via ESLint and the CI pipeline. Carol set up the lint rules. No more any types allowed in Horizon. All new code must pass strict TypeScript. The CI on GitHub now blocks merges on type errors.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text: We adopted LangChain for the AI query interface in Orion. Dave evaluated direct OpenAI API calls vs LangChain â€” went with LangChain for the chain composition and memory management. Pinecone for vector storage.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Frank upgraded the Titan Kubernetes cluster to GKE Autopilot. This reduces the operational overhead of managing node pools. All Docker containers are now scheduled on Autopilot. Cost went down 20% compared to the old node pool setup.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text: The Prism service â€” real-time analytics layer â€” will use Kafka Streams for processing and Postgres for persistence. Bob is leading the Prism design. Alice will review the Postgres schema. Target: Q2 launch.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Hiro joined as the new DevOps engineer supporting Frank on the Titan infrastructure. Hiro has deep Kubernetes and Terraform experience. First project: improving the Docker build pipeline speed with layer caching.

chunks.author: eve
chunks.text: Welcome everyone to Meridian's all-hands kickoff for Q1 2024. We're launching three major projects this quarter: Atlas (our new transaction ledger), Phoenix (event streaming), and Horizon (the customer dashboard). Alice leads Atlas and Phoenix, Carol leads Horizon.

chunks.author: eve
chunks.text: Exciting news: Meridian has closed our Series A. This gives us runway to scale Atlas and Phoenix, and to hire the engineers we need for Orion. Dave is starting on Orion next month. Thank you to everyone who contributed to the fundraise.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: Reminder: All Q1 architectural decisions need to be documented in Confluence. Alice has set up the Engineering space. Use the ADR template for any decision involving the core stack â€” Postgres, Kafka, Kubernetes, or TypeScript.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text: The Atlas v0.1 is live in staging. Postgres backend, FastAPI layer, Redis cache. Alice and Grace are doing the final load testing this week. The target is 10k transactions per second before we go to production.

chunks.text: Phoenix Kafka migration is done. Bob's team completed the full switch from RabbitMQ to Kafka ahead of schedule. The event streaming is now running at 600k messages per second. Big win for the infrastructure team.

chunks.author: eve
chunks.text: Meridian company values reminder: we build for reliability first. Every architectural decision â€” from choosing Postgres over MongoDB, to Kafka over RabbitMQ â€” is made with long-term reliability in mind. Not just what's trendy.

chunks.author: eve

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text: Frank has signed the GCP enterprise agreement. Committed spend saves 30% on our Kubernetes and Postgres compute costs. This significantly improves the Atlas unit economics. The GCP deal was approved by the board.

chunks.text: The board has approved the v2 roadmap. Key milestones: Horizon v2 (Carol, Q2), Prism GA (Morgan, Q2), Orion GA (Dave, Q2), Nexus gateway (Frank, Q3), Vega analytics (Alice, Q3), international (Frank, Q4). Nathan has the commercial targets.

chunks.author: eve
chunks.text: Eve here with the Q1 product priorities: 1) Atlas transaction ledger to production with Postgres, 2) Horizon beta launch with TypeScript/React dashboard, 3) Phoenix Kafka migration complete, 4) Orion ML alpha for internal testing.

chunks.source_name: MER-001: Atlas Postgres connection pool tuning for 12k TPS
chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Atlas Postgres connection pool tuning for 12k TPS

Alice: We need to tune PgBouncer connection pool settings for Atlas production. Current config: 50 max connections, causing contention at peak load. Target: increase to 100 max connections in transaction mode. Work with Frank to update the Kubernetes configmap. Redis cache hit rate should absorb the additional read load.

chunks.source_name: MER-002: Phoenix: Migrate RabbitMQ consumers to Kafka
chunks.author: bob
chunks.text:
Phoenix: Migrate RabbitMQ consumers to Kafka

Bob: Full migration of Phoenix event consumers from RabbitMQ to Apache Kafka. RabbitMQ hit 80k msg/s ceiling in Q4 load test. Kafka target: 600k msg/s with 3-broker cluster on Kubernetes. Create Kafka topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). Replication factor: 3. KRaft mode (no Zookeeper). Coordinate with Alice on Atlas event schema.

chunks.source_name: MER-003: Horizon TypeScript strict mode migration
chunks.author: carol

Indexes: B-tree on (status, created_at), GIN on JSONB metadata. Grace to review schema before migration from SQLite prototype.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Atlas Project Overview

Atlas is Meridian's core transaction ledger service, launched in Q1 2024. It is built on Postgres for ACID-compliant transaction processing. Alice is the project lead. Bob handles the Phoenix Kafka event stream integration. The Atlas Postgres database handles all financial transactions with full ACID compliance â€” this was a deliberate decision over MongoDB, which was rejected for its eventual consistency model.

Architecture: Postgres primary (writes), Postgres read replica (reporting), Redis cache (hot data), Elasticsearch (full-text search). API: FastAPI with both REST and GraphQL endpoints. Authentication: OAuth and JWT.

Q1 milestones: Postgres schema locked, 12k TPS in production, integrated with Phoenix Kafka, Horizon dashboard beta launched. Q2 targets: 50k TPS, Elasticsearch integration, Apex export feature.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Q1 2024 Architecture Decision Records

ADR-001: Postgres over MongoDB for Atlas
Decision: We chose Postgres over MongoDB for the Atlas transaction ledger. Rationale: ACID compliance is non-negotiable for financial transactions. MongoDB's eventual consistency model was evaluated and rejected. Alice led the two-week evaluation. The team agreed on Postgres unanimously.

ADR-002: Kafka over RabbitMQ for Phoenix
Decision: We migrated Phoenix event streaming from RabbitMQ to Kafka. Rationale: RabbitMQ hit its throughput ceiling at 80k messages per second. Kafka scales horizontally to 600k+ msg/s. Bob led the Phoenix migration.

ADR-003: TypeScript standardization for Horizon
Decision: All frontend code at Meridian will use TypeScript. No plain JavaScript in the codebase. Carol led the initiative after the maintenance pain of mixed JS/TS in the early Horizon codebase. ESLint enforces TypeScript strict mode.

ADR-004: PyTorch over TensorFlow for Orion
Decision: Orion ML service uses PyTorch. Dave evaluated both frameworks â€” PyTorch's dynamic computation graph and the team's existing expertise made it the clear choice.

ADR-005: Kubernetes on GCP for Titan
Decision: All Meridian services run on Kubernetes on Google Cloud Platform. Frank migrated from AWS EC2. GCP enterprise agreement signed. GKE Autopilot reduces node pool management overhead.

chunks.author: bob
chunks.text:
Phoenix Event Streaming Architecture

Phoenix is Meridian's event streaming pipeline built on Kafka. Bob leads Phoenix. The migration from RabbitMQ to Kafka was completed in Q1 2024 after RabbitMQ topped out at 80k messages per second during the Q4 load test. Kafka now handles 600k+ messages per second with horizontal scalability.

Kafka cluster: 3 brokers on Kubernetes (Titan), KRaft mode (no Zookeeper). Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). Replication factor: 3.

Consumers: Atlas uses Kafka for event sourcing, Orion consumes Atlas events for ML training data, Horizon subscribes to Kafka topics for real-time dashboard updates via WebSockets. All consumer groups have dedicated lag monitoring in Grafana.

Phoenix integration with Prism: Bob designed the Kafka Streams layer that feeds Prism's real-time analytics. The stream processing aggregates Atlas transaction events in real-time.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text:
Horizon Dashboard Product Spec

Horizon is the customer-facing dashboard for the Meridian platform. Carol leads the product and engineering. Built with React and TypeScript throughout â€” this was a deliberate decision standardized on TypeScript in Q1 2024.

Stack: React (frontend framework), TypeScript (language), GraphQL (external API), REST (internal services), Kafka WebSocket (real-time updates), Radix UI (component primitives), recharts (data visualization).

Key features (v1): Transaction history from Atlas Postgres, real-time Kafka event notifications, Orion ML prediction panel, Prism analytics charts, Atlas search via Elasticsearch, feature flag management (Redis-backed).

Design: Skyler owns Figma designs, Tyler implements the TypeScript component library in Storybook. All 47 components are documented. The design system uses semantic tokens for Atlas transaction states and Phoenix Kafka states.

V2 roadmap: international localization (i18next, Q4), mobile PWA improvements, Zenith customer success portal integration, Nexus API gateway integration.

chunks.author: dave
chunks.text:
Orion ML Service Design

Orion is Meridian's machine learning service for transaction prediction and anomaly detection. Dave leads the service, Sam supports ML research.

Stack: PyTorch (model training and inference), FastAPI (service layer), Airflow (training pipeline orchestration on Kubernetes), GCP (T4 GPU instances for training), Google Cloud Storage (model artifacts), Pinecone (vector store), LangChain (natural language query interface, Q3 roadmap).

Decision rationale â€” PyTorch over TensorFlow: Dave evaluated both. PyTorch's dynamic computation graph is better suited for the research-to-production workflow. Team expertise was also a factor â€” 3 of 4 ML team members have prior PyTorch experience.

Current model: 96% prediction accuracy on production dataset (as of Q1 2024). Inference latency: under 60ms via the FastAPI endpoint. The model is trained on Atlas Postgres transaction data consumed via Phoenix Kafka.

Q2 plan: General availability. Q3 plan: custom fine-tuning for enterprise customers. Q4 plan: LangChain integration for natural language queries over Atlas and Phoenix data.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text:
Titan Infrastructure Runbook

Titan is Meridian's infrastructure layer managed by Frank, with Hiro supporting. All services run on Kubernetes on GCP (GKE Autopilot). Docker is used for all service containerization.

Infrastructure: Terraform manages all GCP resources. The Terraform state is in GCS. All environments (dev, staging, production) are defined as Terraform workspaces. Frank is the Terraform owner.

Services on Kubernetes: Atlas (FastAPI, 3 replicas min, HPA to 30), Phoenix (Kafka consumers, 2 replicas min, HPA to 20), Orion (FastAPI, 2 replicas min), Horizon (React SPA on Nginx, 2 replicas min), Nexus (Nginx gateway, 3 replicas). All services have resource requests and limits defined.

Observability: Prometheus scrapes all services. Grafana dashboards for each service. Sentry for error tracking. Datadog APM for Phoenix Kafka consumer performance. PagerDuty for on-call alerting â€” Alice for Atlas, Bob for Phoenix, Carol for Horizon, Dave for Orion, Frank for Titan.

Security: JWT secrets rotated quarterly (next rotation: June 1). GCP IAM roles follow least privilege. OAuth handled by the Nexus gateway. GitHub secrets for CI/CD managed by Frank.

chunks.text:
Prism Real-Time Analytics Spec

Prism is Meridian's real-time analytics layer, led by Morgan. Q2 GA target.

Architecture decision: Kafka Streams for real-time processing, Postgres for persistence. Alice and Bob co-designed the architecture after a debate between batch Postgres queries (Alice's initial proposal) and stream processing (Bob's proposal). Hybrid approach was agreed: Kafka Streams for real-time aggregates, results materialized into Postgres for historical queries.

Data sources: Atlas Postgres (transaction events via Phoenix Kafka), Orion predictions (via Phoenix Kafka topics), Horizon user activity events.

Key features: Real-time transaction volume by category, Orion prediction accuracy trends, Kafka consumer lag visibility, Atlas Postgres write throughput charts. Carol's Horizon TypeScript components render all Prism visualizations.

chunks.text:
Meridian Series A Pitch Deck â€” Technical Architecture

MERIDIAN PLATFORM â€” TECHNICAL ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW
Prepared for Series A investors. Author: Alice (CTO), Eve (CEO).

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Meridian has built a production-grade data platform on Postgres, Kafka, React/TypeScript, and Kubernetes on GCP. Every technical decision was made for reliability and scalability at enterprise scale.

CORE PLATFORM COMPONENTS

1. ATLAS â€” Transaction Ledger (Lead: Alice)
Database: PostgreSQL â€” chosen for ACID compliance. MongoDB was evaluated and rejected due to eventual consistency limitations that are unacceptable for financial transaction data. Atlas handles 12,000 transactions per second in production. The Postgres schema uses UUID primary keys, JSONB for flexible metadata, and comprehensive indexing. PgBouncer connection pooling at 100 connections max. Redis caching layer reduces Postgres load by 40%.

2. PHOENIX â€” Event Streaming (Lead: Bob)
Framework: Apache Kafka â€” migrated from RabbitMQ in Q1 2024. RabbitMQ was hitting its ceiling at 80,000 messages per second. Kafka now handles 600,000 messages per second with 3 brokers in KRaft mode on Kubernetes. Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions). All brokers on Titan Kubernetes cluster on GCP.

3. ORION â€” ML Predictions (Lead: Dave)
Framework: PyTorch â€” chosen over TensorFlow for dynamic computation graph and team expertise. Training on GCP T4 GPU instances, orchestrated by Airflow. Inference via FastAPI: 58ms p99 latency. 96% prediction accuracy on Atlas production data. Vector storage: Pinecone. Q3 roadmap: LangChain integration for natural language queries.

4. HORIZON â€” Customer Dashboard (Lead: Carol)
Stack: React + TypeScript â€” standardized on TypeScript strict mode in Q1 2024 after maintenance issues with mixed JS/TS. GraphQL external API, REST internal APIs, Kafka WebSockets for real-time updates. All 47 UI components typed and documented in Storybook. Design system: Radix UI primitives with custom semantic tokens.

5. TITAN â€” Infrastructure (Lead: Frank)
Orchestration: Kubernetes on GCP (GKE Autopilot). IaC: Terraform. Containers: Docker. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana + Sentry + Datadog. Secrets: GitHub Secrets, rotated quarterly. Cost optimization: GCP enterprise agreement reduces compute spend by 30%.

COMPETITIVE DIFFERENTIATION
Our competitors use MongoDB for their transaction data â€” this has caused publicized data consistency issues for their customers. Our Postgres-based Atlas ledger with ACID compliance is our primary enterprise differentiator, as validated by 18 of 20 customer interviews conducted by Blake and Eve.

chunks.source_name: Meridian Technical Specification â€” Atlas v1.0
chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Meridian Technical Specification â€” Atlas v1.0

ATLAS TRANSACTION LEDGER â€” TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION v1.0
Classification: Internal | Author: Alice | Reviewed by: Bob, Frank

1. OVERVIEW
Atlas is the core transaction ledger for the Meridian platform. It provides ACID-compliant transaction processing backed by PostgreSQL. This specification covers the v1.0 architecture as deployed in production in Q1 2024.

2. TECHNOLOGY DECISIONS

2.1 Database: PostgreSQL
Postgres was chosen after a two-week evaluation against MongoDB in January 2024 (led by Alice). The decision criteria:
- ACID compliance: REQUIRED. Postgres delivers. MongoDB rejected for eventual consistency.
- Write throughput: 10k+ TPS required. Postgres with PgBouncer delivers 12k TPS in production.
- Replication: Postgres streaming replication to read replica for Horizon and Prism.
- Extensions: JSONB for metadata, pg_stat_statements for query analysis.

2.2 Caching: Redis
Redis handles all hot read paths â€” account balance caching, session state, and feature flags for Horizon. Frank provisioned the Redis cluster on GCP. Cache hit rate: 85%+ on account balance queries.

2.3 Search: Elasticsearch
Elasticsearch provides full-text search over the Atlas transaction history. MongoDB's text search was evaluated and rejected (Elasticsearch gives better relevance scoring). Dave integrated Elasticsearch. Alice reviewed the index design.

2.4 Event Streaming: Kafka (Phoenix)
All Atlas writes emit events to the Phoenix Kafka topic (atlas-events, 24 partitions). This enables: Orion ML training data, Horizon real-time notifications, Prism analytics aggregates, Apex export triggers. Bob designed and owns the Kafka integration.

3. API DESIGN
External API: GraphQL (chosen by Carol after evaluation of REST-only approach). Internal APIs: REST via FastAPI. Authentication: OAuth + JWT (validated by the Nexus API gateway). Both REST and GraphQL are documented in Avery's API documentation.

4. PERFORMANCE TARGETS
v1.0 (current): 12k TPS, p99 latency < 5ms, 99.99% uptime SLA.
v2.0 (Q2 target): 50k TPS with Elasticsearch, connection pool optimization, read replica scaling.
v3.0 (Q3 stretch): 100k TPS with Vega OLAP offloading long-running analytical queries from Atlas Postgres.

5. OPERATIONS
Owner: Alice (primary), Grace (secondary).

Monitoring: Prometheus metrics (Grafana dashboards), Sentry error tracking, Datadog APM for query performance.

The monorepo is on GitHub. All services use Docker for local development. PLATFORM OVERVIEW Meridian's platform has five core services: - Atlas: Transaction ledger (Postgres + FastAPI). Lead: Alice. - Phoenix: Event streaming (Kafka + consumers). Lead: Bob. - Orion: ML predictions (PyTorch + FastAPI). Lead: Dave. - Horizon: Customer dashboard (React + TypeScript + GraphQL). Lead: Carol. - Titan: Infrastructure (Kubernetes + Terraform on GCP). Lead: Frank. KEY TECHNICAL DECISIONS TO KNOW 1. We use Postgres for Atlas â€” not MongoDB. This was a deliberate decision for ACID compliance made by Alice in January 2024. If you're coming from a MongoDB background, the ACID model is a core requirement â€” don't propose MongoDB for any Atlas data. 2. We use Kafka for Phoenix â€” migrated from RabbitMQ by Bob in Q1 2024. Kafka handles 600k msg/s. All service integration uses Kafka topics, not direct API calls between services. 3.
