chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-001: Postgres over MongoDB for Atlas
chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
ADR-001: Postgres over MongoDB for Atlas

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-15 | Author: Alice | Reviewers: Bob, Carol, Dave, Frank

Context:
The Atlas transaction ledger requires a reliable, consistent datastore. Two candidates were evaluated: PostgreSQL and MongoDB. The evaluation was led by Alice over two weeks in January 2024.

Evaluation criteria:
- ACID compliance (critical for financial transactions)
- Write throughput at 10k+ TPS
- Operational complexity
- Team familiarity

Decision: PostgreSQL was chosen. MongoDB was rejected.

Rationale:
Postgres provides full ACID compliance including atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability. MongoDB's default eventual consistency model is a dealbreaker for the Atlas transaction ledger â€” partial writes on financial records cannot be tolerated. The team has strong Postgres expertise. Postgres scales to our projected 100k TPS via connection pooling (PgBouncer) and read replicas.

Consequences:
All Atlas transaction writes go to the Postgres primary. A read replica serves the Horizon dashboard and Prism analytics. Redis caches hot read paths. Elasticsearch provides full-text search over the transaction history.

This decision is final. MongoDB shall not be re-evaluated for the Atlas core ledger in this product phase.

chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-002: Kafka over RabbitMQ for Phoenix
chunks.author: bob
chunks.text:
ADR-002: Kafka over RabbitMQ for Phoenix

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-02-01 | Author: Bob | Reviewers: Alice, Frank

Context:
Phoenix, Meridian's event streaming pipeline, was originally built on RabbitMQ. During Q4 2023 load testing, RabbitMQ reached its throughput ceiling at 80,000 messages per second. The Atlas transaction ledger, Orion ML service, and Horizon dashboard all depend on reliable event streaming.

Candidates evaluated: Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ (status quo), AWS SQS.

Decision: Migrate Phoenix from RabbitMQ to Apache Kafka.

Rationale:
Kafka provides horizontal scalability â€” our cluster now handles 600,000 messages per second with 3 brokers. Kafka's log-based storage allows consumers to replay events, which is essential for Orion's ML training pipeline. Kafka's consumer group model gives us independent scaling of Atlas, Orion, and Horizon consumers. AWS SQS was rejected for vendor lock-in reasons; Kafka runs on our GCP Kubernetes infrastructure managed by Frank.

Kafka cluster configuration: 3 brokers, KRaft mode (no Zookeeper), 3x replication. Topics: atlas-events (24 partitions), orion-predictions (12 partitions), horizon-notifications (6 partitions).

This decision was approved by Alice and Frank. The migration was completed in February 2024.

chunks.author: carol
chunks.text:
ADR-003: TypeScript Standardization

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-20 | Author: Carol | Reviewers: Alice, Eve

Context:
The early Horizon codebase mixed JavaScript and TypeScript, causing type errors in production and slowing down development velocity. Carol proposed standardizing on TypeScript strict mode across all Meridian frontend code.

Decision: All Meridian frontend code must use TypeScript. No plain JavaScript is allowed in the Horizon, developer portal, or Zenith codebases.

Rationale:
TypeScript catches type errors at compile time, not runtime. The Horizon dashboard interfaces with the Atlas Postgres data model and the Phoenix Kafka event schema â€” both have strict types. TypeScript ensures these contracts are enforced at the API boundary. Maintenance cost of mixed JS/TS is too high. Carol standardized ESLint with TypeScript strict mode and the CI pipeline now blocks merges on type errors.

Implementation:
- ESLint with TypeScript strict mode configured (no `any` types)
- GitHub CI pipeline blocks on TypeScript errors
- Storybook documentation for all 47 typed React components (Tyler implemented)
- Feature flags backed by Redis, typed with TypeScript interfaces

This decision has been ratified by leadership (Eve) and applies to all current and future frontend engineering at Meridian.

chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-004: PyTorch over TensorFlow for Orion
chunks.author: dave
chunks.text:
ADR-004: PyTorch over TensorFlow for Orion

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-22 | Author: Dave | Reviewers: Alice, Eve

Context:
Orion, the Meridian ML prediction service, needed a deep learning framework. Dave evaluated PyTorch and TensorFlow.

Decision: Orion uses PyTorch for model training and inference.

Rationale:
PyTorch's dynamic computation graph (eager execution) is better suited to Meridian's research-to-production workflow. The team's existing expertise is in PyTorch â€” 3 of 4 ML engineers have prior experience. TensorFlow's static graph model and the complexity of TensorFlow Serving added operational overhead that Frank did not want on the Titan Kubernetes infrastructure.

PyTorch model training runs on GCP T4 GPU instances, orchestrated by Airflow. Model artifacts stored in Google Cloud Storage. Inference via FastAPI endpoint (under 60ms p99). Sam is now running experiments with Pinecone for vector storage and LangChain for the Q3 natural language query feature.

The Orion PyTorch model achieved 96% accuracy on the Atlas production dataset. This decision is final for the current ML architecture phase.

chunks.source_name: Engineering/ADR-005: GCP and Kubernetes for Titan
chunks.author: frank
chunks.text:
ADR-005: GCP and Kubernetes for Titan

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-01-10 | Author: Frank | Reviewers: Alice, Eve

Context:
Meridian's infrastructure was originally on AWS EC2 instances with manual provisioning. Frank proposed migrating to Kubernetes on GCP using Terraform for infrastructure-as-code.

Decision: All Meridian services run on Kubernetes (GKE Autopilot) on Google Cloud Platform. Terraform manages all GCP infrastructure. Docker is the containerization standard.

Rationale:
GCP enterprise agreement saves 30% on compute costs. GKE Autopilot removes node pool management overhead. Terraform ensures reproducible infrastructure â€” all environments (dev, staging, prod) are code. Docker standardizes the build process across all Meridian services (Atlas, Phoenix, Orion, Horizon). AWS was rejected to avoid vendor lock-in after the GCP enterprise discount made GCP the obvious choice.

Horizontal Pod Autoscalers configured for all services. Prometheus + Grafana observability. PagerDuty on-call integration. Hiro joined the Titan team to support this infrastructure at scale.

This decision is ratified by leadership and the GCP enterprise agreement is signed.

chunks.author: alice
chunks.text:
Atlas Postgres Schema Documentation

This page documents the Atlas transaction ledger Postgres schema. Author: Alice. Last updated: March 2024.

Core tables:

transactions: id (UUID PK), amount (DECIMAL), currency (VARCHAR), source_account (UUID FK), destination_account (UUID FK), status (ENUM: pending/processing/complete/failed), created_at (TIMESTAMPTZ), updated_at (TIMESTAMPTZ), metadata (JSONB)

accounts: id (UUID PK), owner_id (UUID FK), account_type (ENUM: checking/savings/business), balance (DECIMAL), created_at (TIMESTAMPTZ)

events: id (UUID PK), transaction_id (UUID FK), event_type (VARCHAR), payload (JSONB), kafka_offset (BIGINT), created_at (TIMESTAMPTZ)

Indexing strategy: B-tree index on transactions(status, created_at) for status queries. GIN index on transactions(metadata) for JSONB queries. The Elasticsearch layer mirrors the transactions table for full-text search.

Connection pooling: PgBouncer in transaction mode, 100 max connections. This was tuned by Alice after load testing. The read replica (for Horizon, Prism, Apex) connects directly without PgBouncer.

Backup: Postgres WAL archiving to GCS. Point-in-time recovery enabled. Daily full backup. Frank owns the backup and recovery runbook in the Titan Confluence space.

chunks.author: frank
chunks.text:
Security and Compliance Policy

Meridian Security Policy â€” owned by Frank and Rachel. Approved by Eve.

Authentication: All Meridian APIs use OAuth 2.0 for authentication and JWT for session tokens. The Nexus API gateway validates JWT on all incoming requests before routing to Atlas, Orion, or Horizon. JWT secrets are rotated quarterly â€” Frank owns the rotation schedule (next rotation: June 1, 2024).

Data residency: Atlas Postgres data for EU customers must remain in GCP europe-west1. Frank is setting up a dedicated EU Kubernetes cluster. Rachel is coordinating the GDPR compliance review with external auditors.

GitHub secrets: All secrets (GCP credentials, Postgres passwords, Kafka authentication, JWT secrets) are stored in GitHub Secrets. Frank manages the secrets rotation. No secrets are committed to code.

Data classification: Production Atlas transaction data must never appear in Slack, Notion, Jira, or any communication channel. Postgres is the only authorized store for production transaction data. Rachel enforces this policy via the incident management process.

Security audit: Scheduled for Q2 2024. Focus: Atlas Postgres encryption at rest, JWT token handling across all services, OAuth flow in Horizon, network policies on the Titan Kubernetes cluster.

chunks.author: morgan
chunks.text:
Prism Architecture Decision

Status: ACCEPTED | Date: 2024-03-01 | Author: Morgan | Reviewers: Alice, Bob

Context:
Meridian needs a real-time analytics layer on top of Atlas transactions and Phoenix events. Morgan proposed Prism as a new service.

Decision: Prism uses Apache Kafka Streams for real-time processing, with results materialized into Postgres for historical queries.

Evaluation:
- Option A: Batch Postgres queries (Alice's initial proposal) â€” simple, but 30-minute lag on aggregate metrics
- Option B: Kafka Streams with Postgres persistence (Bob's proposal) â€” real-time, more complex
- Decision: Option B was chosen. Alice and Bob agreed on the hybrid: Kafka Streams for real-time, Postgres for persistence.

Kafka Streams topology: consumes atlas-events and orion-predictions topics, computes rolling aggregates (1-minute, 1-hour, 1-day), writes to Postgres aggregate tables, which the Horizon React TypeScript dashboard renders via GraphQL.

Morgan leads Prism product. Bob leads Kafka Streams implementation. Alice owns the Postgres schema. Carol owns the Horizon integration (React TypeScript charts using recharts). Q2 GA target confirmed.

chunks.author: rachel
chunks.text: Rachel: security audit scope confirmed. Focus areas: Atlas Postgres encryption at rest (Frank to lead remediation), JWT token handling across all services (Frank), OAuth flow in Horizon (Carol), network policies on Titan Kubernetes (Frank). The audit starts next month. Alice: please make sure the Atlas Postgres config is audit-ready.

chunks.author: eve
chunks.text: Eve: v2 roadmap confirmed by leadership and board. Q2: Atlas 50k TPS, Horizon v2 launch, Prism GA, Orion GA. Q3: Nexus gateway, Vega analytics, Zenith customer portal. Q4: International (GCP europe-west1), LangChain NLQ for Orion, Horizon i18n. Open decisions still needed: v2 pricing model (Eve/Nathan), LangChain backend (Dave/leadership), Vega: Postgres vs BigQuery.
